title: Emergence of Localized Surface Plasmons in Unpatterned Hyperdoped Polycrystalline Silicon
authors: Mohamad Bahsoun, Jesse Groenen, Gonzague Agez, Sébastien Joulié, Cécile Marcelot, Robin Cours, Sébastien Kerdiles, Mathieu Opprecht, Caroline Bonafos, Jean-Marie Poumirol
abstract: The ability to engineer localized surface plasmon resonances at large scale usually relies on precise nanoscale patterning. Here, we demonstrate that mid-infrared plasmonic responses can instead emerge in unpatterned polysilicon films composed of nanometric (5-50 nm) grains, challenging established design paradigms and eliminating the need for external nanostructuring. Using tailored out-of-equilibrium annealing conditions, we show that hyperdoped polysilicon layers exhibit enhanced light-matter interactions that can be tuned across the mid-infrared range. By combining advanced electron microscopy, infrared spectroscopy and finite-difference time-domain electrodynamic simulations, we demonstrate that these remarkable optical properties originate from naturally formed metal-dielectric interfaces at grain boundaries, which support localized surface plasmon resonances. Importantly, this result is universal and can be extended to any doped semiconductor system, regardless of the synthesis technique, provided that the grain size remains in the nanometric range. This work opens up a new field in plasmonics centered on polycrystalline semiconductors, paving the way for cost-effective systems that are fully compatible with microelectronic and photovoltaic technologies, and capable of significantly reshaping light-matter interactions in the infrared range.
summary: Researchers have demonstrated that localized surface plasmon resonances can emerge in unpatterned, hyperdoped polycrystalline silicon without the need for lithographic patterning. By using laser annealing to create nanometric grains, the grain boundaries naturally function as dielectric gaps between metallic-like silicon domains. This approach offers a scalable and cost-effective way to create mid-infrared plasmonic devices compatible with existing microelectronics.
main_problem: The difficulty of mass-manufacturing mid-infrared plasmonic metasurfaces due to the reliance on expensive and time-consuming nanoscale patterning.
main_result: The discovery that unpatterned, hyperdoped polycrystalline silicon exhibits localized surface plasmon resonances (LSPR) because grain boundaries act as natural metal-dielectric interfaces.
method: The study combines laser thermal annealing (LTA) for fabrication, HRTEM and ACOM for microstructural analysis, FTIR spectroscopy for optical response, and FDTD simulations for electrodynamic modeling.
model_or_system: The system consists of hyperdoped polycrystalline silicon thin films where nanometric grains (5-50 nm) are separated by ultrathin (0.5-2.5 nm) insulating grain boundaries, acting as a collection of metallic antennas.
key_observables: Normalized transmission spectra, resonance frequency, resonance energy, extinction amplitude, and electron scattering time.
important_parameters: Grain size (5-50 nm), carrier density (up to 1.01e21 cm^-3), filling factor, and annealing temperature relative to the full melt threshold.
main_assumptions: The system operates in a regime where the quasi-static approximation is invalid due to strong dipole-dipable coupling between nanodomains.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows microstructural evolution via HRTEM; Fig 2 shows grain orientation maps; Fig 3 displays experimental transmission spectra showing the transition from Drude-like to LSPR behavior; Fig 4 presents FDTD simulations of antenna arrays varying gap size, antenna size, and randomness.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scaling problem of plasmonics, describes the fabrication of hyperdoped polysilicon, presents microstructural characterization, details the optical spectroscopy results, and concludes with FDTD simulations to explain the physical mechanism of emergent LSPR.
why_interesting: The paper demonstrates how structural disorder and grain boundaries can be leveraged to create emergent optical resonances, which is relevant to understanding light-matter interaction in disordered nanophotonic environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Microstructural evolution at the melting threshold. High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) images in cross-section of samples D1 and D2 after LTA: before Full Melt (FM)(a) and (d), at FM (b) and (e),(f), after FM (c) and (g). In inset, associated Fast Fourier Transforms of the images. The laser energy densities at FM for D1 and D2 are given in Table S1(SI). For the two doses, the hyperdoped Si top layer is monocrystalline below FM and polycrystalline beyond FM. At the FM onset, highly twinned silicon layers are obtained, together with polycrystalline regions for D2.

Figure 2: Orientation maps and grain-size distributions of polysilicon layers. ACOM images (orientation maps) of D1 ((a),(c)) and D2 ((b), (d)) annealed beyond the FM threshold, in cross-section (XS) and plan-view (PV); (e), (f), (g) and (h) associated size-distributions. The hyperdoped polycrystalline layer consists of randomly oriented nanograins with sizes ranging between 5 and 50 nm for both D1 and D2.

Figure 3: Experimental optical response of hyper-doped Si layers. Normalized transmittance mea- sured for D1 (a) and D2 (b). Continuous lines, measured on unpatterned hyper-doped Si layers below full melt, at full melt and beyond full melt. Dashed lines, simulated using model described in the text. For samples annealed below FM, the normalized transmittance remains close to unity at high frequencies and decreases markedly at low frequencies, consistent with free-electron absorption in the n-doped layer (Drude- like response). In contrast, polycrystalline doped Si layers exhibit a pronounced transmission minimum associated with a well-defined resonance that blueshifts with increasing dopant...

Figure 4: FDTD-calculated transmission for doped Si antenna arrays. a. FDTD calculated normal- ized transmission for doped Si quadratic antenna in infinite square array, with dimension of a =15 nm, b = 15 nm, c = 23 nm with carrier density N=1×1021cm−3 and scattering time τ = 3.4 × 10−15s. Antennas are separated by a variable thickness d (from 0 nm to 15 nm) of amorphous Si and deposited on SiO2. Insert: schematic configuration used for numerical simulations. Incoming light polarization is fixed as illustrated by the arrow b. Calculated normalized transmission as in (a) but for a constant values of d = 0.5 nm and various values of antennas size (a = b= 3, 23, 40, 100 nm). c. Calculated...
